question: How does Content Security Policy (CSP) protect against XSS?

options.text: CSP controls where the browser can load scripts from -- inline scripts and scripts from unauthorized domains are blocked
CSP encrypts scripts before execution in the browser
options.explanation: CSP doesn't encrypt anything. It tells the browser: 'only execute scripts from these domains'. If XSS injects a <script> tag, it's blocked since inline scripts are forbidden

options.text: CSP removes dangerous HTML tags from the server response
options.explanation: CSP doesn't modify HTML. It's a header directive for the browser. HTML sanitization is server code's job (DOMPurify, xss). CSP is the last line of defense

options.text: CSP blocks all JavaScript on the page
options.explanation: CSP doesn't block all JS. It allows scripts from the whitelist (script-src 'self' cdn.com). Only scripts from unauthorized sources and inline scripts are blocked

question: Why are SameSite cookies the best CSRF protection for modern APIs?

options.text: SameSite: strict/lax prevents the browser from sending cookies with cross-origin requests -- evil.com won't get bank.com's cookie
SameSite encrypts the cookie so attackers can't read it
options.explanation: SameSite doesn't encrypt (that's what Secure flag + HTTPS does). SameSite controls WHEN the cookie is sent -- not with cross-origin requests

options.text: SameSite automatically generates a CSRF token
options.explanation: SameSite doesn't generate tokens. It replaces the need for CSRF tokens by simply not sending cookies on cross-origin requests

options.text: SameSite makes the cookie invisible to JavaScript
options.explanation: That's the httpOnly flag, not SameSite. SameSite controls cookie sending in cross-origin requests. httpOnly protects from XSS (document.cookie)

question: What is NoSQL injection and how do you protect against it?

options.text: Passing MongoDB operators ($gt, $ne) through input -- defense: express-mongo-sanitize (strips $ and .) + Zod validation
SQL injection in MongoDB -- same defense as SQL databases
options.explanation: MongoDB doesn't use SQL. NoSQL injection passes MongoDB operators via JSON. { email: { $gt: '' } } returns the first user instead of a specific one

options.text: Injecting JavaScript into MongoDB via eval
options.explanation: While $where and eval are dangerous, typical NoSQL injection passes operators ($gt, $ne, $exists) via unchecked input. express-mongo-sanitize solves this

options.text: NoSQL injection is impossible because MongoDB doesn't use SQL
options.explanation: Absence of SQL doesn't mean security. MongoDB operators ($gt, $ne, $regex) allow bypassing filters. { password: { $ne: '' } } bypasses password checks

options.text: Nested quantifiers (a+)+ create exponential backtracking paths on strings like 'aaaaab'
It's an infinite loop -- the regex never terminates
options.explanation: It's not infinite -- the regex will terminate, but on a 25-character string it takes minutes/hours. The number of backtracks grows exponentially: 2^n

options.text: Regex consumes lots of RAM when processing long strings
options.explanation: ReDoS is a CPU problem, not RAM. The regex engine tries exponential paths, loading the processor. This enables DoS via a single HTTP string

options.text: Only in Node.js -- other languages aren't affected by ReDoS
options.explanation: ReDoS affects all languages with backtracking regex engines: JavaScript, Python, Java, Ruby, .NET. Only RE2 (Go, Rust) uses a linear-time algorithm

question: Which headers does Helmet set by default?

options.text: X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff -- prevents MIME sniffing
X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN -- clickjacking protection
Authorization: Bearer -- automatic JWT authentication
options.explanation: Helmet doesn't manage authentication. Authorization is a client request header, not a server response. Helmet sets security headers in responses

options.text: Strict-Transport-Security -- enforces HTTPS
